frozen_components: dataflow transduction rule set (declaratively authored, not learned)
QCFG grammar structure (derived from rules, not trained)
architecture.summary: A hybrid dialogue response generation system combining rule-based content selection with neural language modeling. Dataflow transduction rules map executable dataflow graphs (representing dialogue agent actions and results) into quasi-synchronous context-free grammars (QCFGs) that specify the space of truthful, contextually acceptable responses. A constrained decoding procedure then performs beam search to select fluent utterances that satisfy both the QCFG constraints and maximize probability under a pretrained neural language model, effectively intersecting grammar-based content control with LM-based fluency.
trained_components: neural language model (CodeT5-base, fine-tuned on task-specific examples)

biblio.title: The Whole Truth and Nothing But the Truth: Faithful and Controllable Dialogue Response Generation with Dataflow Transduction and Constrained Decoding

claims.statement: QCFG-constrained decoding achieves 100% truthfulness on 297 randomly sampled SMCalFlow validation examples in human evaluation, whereas unconstrained LM decoding achieves 82%
QCFG-constrained decoding with 3% training data (268 examples) achieves BLEU scores on par with unconstrained decoding using 100% of training data

baseline.name: QCFG random sampling and unconstrained decoding

claims.statement: On SMCalFlow validation data, QCFG random sampling achieves 100% truthfulness but only 56% relevance; unconstrained decoding achieves 97% relevance but 82% truthfulness; QCFG-constrained decoding achieves 97% relevance and 100% truthfulness
On MultiWOZ 2.1 (100 test examples manually evaluated), QCFG-constrained decoding produces truthful responses on all 100 examples; MTTOD baseline achieves 89 truthful responses
187 dataflow transduction rules, written by authors in a matter of hours, were sufficient to cover all gold system responses in the SMCalFlow calendar event query subset (8938 training examples)
The system outperforms both rule-based (QCFG random sampling) and end-to-end learned approaches (unconstrained LM decoding) in human evaluations of fluency, relevance, and truthfulness
Even QCFG-constrained decoding produces pragmatically unhelpful truthful omissions in a few cases, e.g., 'Looks like it' instead of more specific response
Authoring dataflow transduction rules is labor-intensive for complex domains, and the approach has only been tested on dataflow graphs and English
